retrieved_docs.title: Immune Thrombocytopenia -- History and Physical -- History
retrieved_docs.content: Possible secondary causes must be investigated. For example, about 60% of children diagnosed with ITP have a preceding viral illness within the past month. [19] Studies have also shown a small increased ITP risk within 6 weeks after vaccination with the measles, mumps, and rubella vaccine. ITP has also been reported after varicella, hepatitis A, and tetanus-diphtheria-acellular pertussis vaccinations in older children. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: MMR Vaccine -- Adverse Effects -- Rubella-Containing Vaccines
retrieved_docs.content: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura: A rare adverse reaction to the MMR or MMRV vaccine is immune thrombocytopenic purpura, which may occur within 6 weeks of immunization. In most children, immune thrombocytopenic purpura resolves within 3 months without complication. In these cases, a serologic evaluation can be performed to determine the need for an additional dose. Clinicians should consider the risks of another adverse event of immune thrombocytopenic purpura before a second dose of the vaccine is required. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: ITP-Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Immune thrombocytopenic purpura can occur with infections (e.g., human immunodeficiency virus), malignancy (e.g., adenocarcinoma and lymphoma), and common variable immunodeficiency and autoimmune diseases (e.g., systemic lupus erythematosus, autoimmune hepatitis, and thyroid disease). [4]

retrieved_docs.title: ITP-Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Azathioprine may take several months of therapy to exert an effect on ITP. [9] Although deemed safe in pregnancy, there are reports of an increased rate of prematurity as well as a decreased birth rate. [13] In general, vaccinations must be withheld for six months after the total cessation of immunosuppressive therapy to allow for the restoration of immunity.

retrieved_docs.title: ITP-Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who do not respond adequately and have active bleeding after a month of being treated with corticosteroids may need splenectomy after using intravenous immunoglobulins to raise the platelet count. Therefore, splenectomy is the treatment of choice for adult patients with ITP who have persistent symptomatic thrombocytopenia. It is recommended that patients be screened for COVID-19 prior to splenectomy. [13] Splenectomy results in upwards of a 70% long-term response rate; it is important to give vaccinations > 15 days prior to surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: ITP-Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Childhood immune thrombocytopenic purpura often occurs within a few weeks following a viral infection, suggesting a possible cross-immunization between viral and platelet antigens, the absorption of immune complexes, or a hapten mechanism. Many other platelet antigens are a target of autoantibodies, including GPIIb/IIIA and GP V (after chickenpox). However, their exact role in diagnostic testing is dubious at best. [3]

retrieved_docs.content: Testing for HIV and Hepatitis C is advocated as these are treatable causes of thrombocytopenia. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: MMR Vaccine -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Anamnesis with thrombocytopenia may show an increased risk of clinically significant thrombocytopenia after immunization with MMR or MMRV. Therefore, decisions on the first dose of MMR for children with a history of thrombocytopenia must consider the risks and benefits. In general, the benefits outweigh the risks. Factors to be evaluated when making this decision include: Relapse of thrombocytopenia after MMR in patients with a history of thrombocytopenia (related and unrelated to the vaccine) Probability of the child's exposure to measles, mumps, and rubella (eg, planned trips abroad, the epidemiology in the place of residence or stay of the child)

retrieved_docs.title: ITP-Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (Archived) -- Differential Diagnosis -- Thrombocytopenias due to increased platelet destruction include:
retrieved_docs.content: Viral infection often is the presage for thrombocytopenia. The HIV-associated disease is now the most common cause of thrombocytopenic purpura, especially in males between 20 and 50. Testing for HIV antibodies is a critical part of the assessment of ITP. [27] [28] Zidovudine effectively raises platelet counts in individuals with HIV-associated immune thrombocytopenic purpura. Data has also shown that COVID is a cause of thrombocytopenia, with 71% of COVID-induced ITP occurring in elderly patients and three-quarters of these having moderate to severe infections. [3] Typically, the platelet count does not drop below 100,000 /dL, severe bleeding is uncommon, and the overall prognosis is deemed relatively favorable. Incidentally, COVID-19 vaccines have been implicated in causing and exacerbating ITP [29] . Hepatitis C can cause significant thrombocytopenia, which can resolve with antiviral treatment. [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.content: Toxicological considerations also extend to the development, evaluation, and administration of viral vaccines, requiring vigilance for any toxic risks associated with vaccine components or adjuvants. Comprehensive awareness of the toxic profiles of these agents, together with systematic application of the Allowed qualifier, is essential for optimizing patient care and ensuring the safety of therapeutic and preventive interventions.

retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease is administered through the injection or infusion of therapeutic or prophylactic substances and is classified as a child procedure within the broader category of such therapeutic injections or infusions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 73
retrieved_docs.content: disorders are placed under the broader category of blood‑globulin deficiency (نقص غلوبيولين الدم), indicating that globulin deficiency underlies or is related to various immune system abnormalities. Recognizing malaise, fatigue, and the link between immunity disorders and globulin deficiency, together with the shared pharmacologic strategies and contraindications, supports an integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approach in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1825
retrieved_docs.content: Immunity disorders are classified as a subset of نقص غلوبيولين الدم, which refers to a deficiency of blood globulins. This globulin deficiency results in reduced levels of immunoglobulins, compromising the body’s immune defenses and predisposing individuals to infections and other immune‑related complications. Recognizing immunity disorders within the broader context of نقص غلوبيولين الدم helps clinicians understand the underlying immunological deficit and guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies aimed at restoring adequate immune function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2570
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease is organized hierarchically to include human vaccination, and human vaccination in turn encompasses التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Injection of RhD immune globulin represents a specific form of التمنيع السلبي, illustrating that this RhD immune‑globulin injection is an example of passive immunization used within the broader context of human vaccination strategies for autoimmune disease prevention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease includes human vaccination as a subordinate concept, indicating that human vaccination is regarded as a specific form of immunization employed to prevent or modulate autoimmune disease in humans.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1759
retrieved_docs.content: Prophylactic vaccination or inoculation against certain bacterial diseases is classified as a therapeutic intervention and is a subcategory of the broader group termed Therapeutic Interventions: Immunization, Injections, Perfusion. This relationship indicates that preventive vaccination against bacterial pathogens is considered part of the overall therapeutic strategies that include various forms of immunization, injectable treatments, and perfusion techniques, highlighting its role within comprehensive patient care aimed at preventing infection.

Collectively, these relationships delineate the major antiviral drug classes, their individual members, therapeutic versus preventive applications, structural and mechanistic attributes, and critical contraindications and drug‑interaction considerations essential for safe and effective patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 55

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2276
retrieved_docs.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1591
retrieved_docs.content: Immunization for autoimmune disease encompasses human vaccination as a specific form of immunization, indicating that vaccines administered to humans can be used to prevent or modulate autoimmune conditions. Human vaccination is situated within the broader category of prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations, reflecting its role as a preventive intervention designed to protect individuals from infectious agents. Prophylactic vaccinations and inoculations functions as the overarching classification that groups together all preventive vaccine strategies, including human vaccination, and thereby provides the hierarchical framework linking general preventive immunization concepts to the more specific application of immunization for autoimmune disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 26
retrieved_docs.content: delineate the structural diversity, contraindications, prophylactic and therapeutic applications of penicillin‑type antibiotics, and the associated laboratory and immunological findings essential for informed clinical decision‑making and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 2235
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of RhD immune globulin is classified as a form of التمنيع السلبي (passive immunization). Within the broader framework of human vaccination, التمنيع السلبي represents a child category, indicating that passive immunization is considered a subset of vaccination strategies applied to humans. Consequently, the administration of RhD immune globulin exemplifies how passive immunization techniques are incorporated into human vaccination programs to provide immediate, short‑term protection, particularly in contexts such as preventing hemolytic disease of the newborn. This relationship underscores the role of passive immunization as an integral component of comprehensive vaccination approaches in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: Viral antigens constitute the active ingredient of viral vaccines, providing the essential immunogenic component that stimulates the body’s immune response and confers protection against the targeted viral infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 76
retrieved_docs.content: The DTPP vaccine is classified as a member of the combined bacterial and viral vaccines group, meaning it incorporates antigens that protect against both bacterial infections (such as diphtheria, tetanus, and pertussis) and viral infections (such as poliomyelitis). By belonging to this category of combination vaccines, the DTPP vaccine enables simultaneous immunization against multiple pathogens, streamlining vaccination schedules and improving patient adherence to preventive care.
